ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'corn soup' entry; no specific Indo-Chinese sweet corn vegetable soup entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific Indo-Chinese sweet corn soup code; 'Soups and broths' (A00GG) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for Indo-Chinese sweet corn vegetable soup.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Sweet Corn Vegetable Soup is approximately 250 ml (one bowl), which is the typical portion served as a starter or side dish in Indian restaurants and homes.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Indo-Chinese Sweet Corn Vegetable Soup exists in ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for sweet corn, mixed vegetables, and cornstarch-thickened preparations, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) entries for sweet corn (USDA #11167) and mixed vegetable soup components. Sodium reflects typical restaurant-style preparation with added salt.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Indo-Chinese Sweet Corn Vegetable Soup. GI of 55 is estimated based on sweet corn (GI ~52–55, University of Sydney GI Database) as the dominant carbohydrate contributor, with cornstarch thickener contributing moderate GI elevation. Dietary fibre from mixed vegetables moderates the overall GI. Per University of Sydney GI classification, GI ≤55 is categorised as 'low'. Glycemic load calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (55 × 26.25) / 100 ≈ 14.4 per 250 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (280 mg per 100 g; ~700 mg per 250 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit intake of this soup due to its high sodium content (~700 mg per serving), which represents approximately 30% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg; a low-sodium home-prepared version with reduced salt and no MSG is preferable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low-to-medium glycemic index (55) driven by sweet corn and cornstarch thickener with a glycemic load of ~14 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this soup in controlled portions (ideally ≤250 ml) as the glycemic load of ~14 per serving can cause moderate postprandial blood glucose elevation; reducing or omitting the cornstarch thickener and increasing vegetable content will lower the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat content with low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the high sodium content (~700 mg per serving) is the primary concern and warrants portion control or home preparation with minimal salt; the low saturated fat content (0.4 g per 100 g) and presence of vegetable-derived antioxidants (vitamin A, vitamin C) are relatively favourable for cardiovascular health.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sweet Corn Vegetable Soup (also known as Sweet Corn Veg Soup or स्वीट कॉर्न वेज सूप in Hindi) is a popular Indo-Chinese soup widely served across India as a starter or side dish. It is prepared by simmering sweet corn kernels (both whole and creamed) with finely chopped mixed vegetables such as carrots, green beans, and spring onions in a seasoned broth, then thickening the mixture with a cornstarch slurry to achieve its characteristic thick, slightly glossy consistency. The soup is seasoned with salt and white pepper, giving it a mild sweet-savory flavor with a creamy mouthfeel. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 65 kcal, 1.5 g protein, 2.2 g fat, and 10.5 g carbohydrates, with 1.8 g dietary fibre and 280 mg sodium. It is a vegetarian, vegan, and gluten-free dish. Clinically, the low GI (55) and high sodium content (~700 mg per 250 ml serving) make it a moderate concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension. It is best consumed in controlled portions and is suitable as a light, warming comfort food during colder months or as a kid-friendly snack.
common_user_queries: What is sweet corn veg soup?
Calories in sweet corn vegetable soup
Is sweet corn soup good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Indo-Chinese Sweet Corn Vegetable Soup exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN sweet corn and mixed vegetable data cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy sweet corn entries. GI is an analogous estimate based on sweet corn GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: water_g set to -1: moisture content for this prepared soup formulation is not directly reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) for this specific dish; estimated water content would be high (~85%) but is not confirmed by an approved source and is therefore set to -1 per numeric hygiene rules. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: this is a plant-based vegetarian soup with no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally zero in plant foods. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero in this plant-based preparation with no fortification. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as no hydrogenated fats are used. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a vegan preparation. Sodium value of 280 mg per 100 g reflects typical restaurant-style preparation; home-prepared versions may have significantly lower sodium. Portions input contained three duplicate entries for '1 serving (250 ml)'; deduplicated to a single default serving of 250 g. GI of 55 is classified as 'low' per University of Sydney GI Database classification (GI ≤55 = low). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (55 × 10.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2.5) / 100 = 14.4 per 250 g serving. Ontology: FoodOn 'corn soup' (FOODON_03315763) used as closest exact match; FoodEx2 'Soups and broths' (A00GG) used as closest_parent category anchor with gap note. No SNOMED CT code identified.
